He didn’t leave for the money. He left because I never gave him the one thing that would have made him stay.

10 min read
Just now

--

Marcus gave notice on a Thursday afternoon.
I remember it was Thursday because I had just finished a one-on-one with him forty minutes earlier. Forty minutes where he told me things were going well. That he was enjoying the architecture work on the new payments service. That he was excited about the system design interview loop he had coming up at a company he mentioned almost casually, the way you mention something you don’t expect to become important.
I said good luck. I meant it generically.
Forty minutes later he was in my office again. Different posture. Different energy. The offer was strong, he said. He had decided to take it. He wanted to give me the full four weeks.
I thanked him. I said the right things. I asked if there was anything that would change his mind.
He paused for a moment that felt longer than it probably was.
“Honestly? If you had told me a year ago what you apparently saw in me, I probably wouldn’t have been looking.”
I didn’t know what to say. So I said I wasn’t sure what he meant.
“You recommended me for the architecture review panel. You pulled me into the incident post-mortem last quarter as the lead. You started cc’ing me on the engineering direction emails. I noticed all of it. I just didn’t know if you noticed what you were doing, or if it was just convenient. So I went somewhere that told me explicitly.”
He wasn’t angry. That was the part that stayed with me. He was just honest, in the way that people are honest when the outcome is already decided and there’s nothing left to protect.
I wrote down what he said in my phone that evening. I have read it more times than I want to admit.
The Invisible Promotion That Nobody Named
Here is what I had been doing with Marcus for fourteen months without realizing I was doing it.
I had been treating him like a senior engineer. Giving him senior scope. Pulling him into senior conversations. Routing the hard architectural questions through him. Asking him to lead the post-mortems that mattered.
I had not told him any of this was deliberate. I had not told him I thought he was operating at a senior level. I had not told him I had been building a case for his promotion, quietly, in the background, waiting for the right cycle.
In my head, this was responsible management. You don’t tell someone they’re getting promoted before you’re certain. You don’t raise expectations you can’t guarantee. You build the case first. You make the announcement when you can back it up.
In Marcus’s head, something different was happening. He was being given more work. More responsibility. More scope. With no signal about whether this was recognition or just convenience. Whether he was being developed or being used. Whether the organization saw what he was doing or simply expected it.
The gap between those two interpretations is not a small gap. It is the gap between an engineer who stays and one who updates his resume.
And I had left him sitting in that gap for fourteen months while I built my case.
What Engineers Actually Need to Stay
I have had this conversation with a lot of engineers since Marcus left. Not the exit interview version. The honest version, the one you can have when the person still works for you and still trusts you enough to say something true.
The pattern that emerges from those conversations is consistent enough that I have stopped being surprised by it.
Engineers do not leave primarily for money. Compensation matters and it needs to be fair. But engineers who leave for money were already leaving for something else. The competing offer gave them permission and a number to name.
What engineers actually leave for is invisible to most managers because it does not appear on any dashboard and does not surface in status updates.
They leave because they do not know where they stand.
Not in the vague, anxiety-driven way. In the specific, operational way. They do not know whether the organization sees what they are doing as good work or expected work. They do not know whether the scope they are operating at is recognized as above their level or assumed as part of their role. They do not know whether the investment they are making in the organization is being noted and will compound or whether it is simply being consumed.
This uncertainty is not paralyzing in the short term. Engineers absorb it. They keep working. They tell themselves the work speaks for itself. They wait.
But the uncertainty accumulates. And at some point, when a recruiter reaches out or a conversation at a conference makes them realize what their work looks like from the outside, they do the thing they have been postponing. They test the market. And the market, which has no reason to be strategic about the timing of its feedback, tells them immediately and explicitly what their current employer has been strategic about withholding.
That is usually when you hear about it.
The Conversation I Should Have Had
I have thought many times about what the conversation with Marcus should have looked like.
Not the version where I promise him a promotion I cannot guarantee. That is still a mistake, just a different one.
The version where I name what I see.
It does not require a title. It does not require an offer. It requires specificity, which is the one thing most managers consistently fail to provide when talking about performance.
The conversation I should have had with Marcus looked something like this.
I want to tell you something I have been observing and have not said explicitly. The way you approached the database migration last quarter, specifically the decision to phase the cutover instead of doing it in one window, that was senior-level operational judgment. I notice that you have been doing this kind of thinking consistently. You are operating above your current level in ways that I think you may not realize I see.
I am building toward a promotion case. I cannot promise the timing. But I want you to know that the scope I am giving you is deliberate, not convenient. You are being developed, not just used.
That conversation would have taken seven minutes.
I did not have it because I was waiting until I could back it up with a guarantee. By the time I could have backed it up, Marcus had already decided not to wait.
The Recognition Gap That Managers Don’t See
There is a specific cognitive error that most engineering managers make, and I made it with Marcus, and I have watched other managers make it repeatedly.
The error is assuming that because you see what an engineer is doing, the engineer knows you see it.
Visibility and communication are not the same thing.
I saw Marcus’s operational judgment. I saw his architecture thinking. I saw him doing senior work consistently. I saw all of it and I was building toward a case.
But I never told him I saw it. I assumed the scope I was giving him was sufficient signal. I assumed that being pulled into the important conversations would communicate to him that he was trusted at that level. I assumed that actions spoke loudly enough that words were optional.
They are not optional. This is the mistake.
Engineers are working in organizations where many signals are ambiguous. Being given more scope can mean you are trusted. It can also mean you are available and reliable and the task needed to be assigned to someone. Being included in important conversations can mean your perspective is valued. It can also mean you happened to be in the right place.
The engineer who has not been told explicitly what the senior engineer who has been told explicitly do not have the same experience of the same working environment, even if every external fact about their situation is identical.
The senior engineer who has been told explicitly knows where they stand. They can plan. They can invest deliberately. They can have a conversation with a recruiter from a position of information rather than uncertainty.
The engineer who has not been told explicitly is making decisions in an information vacuum. And when they fill that vacuum with information from outside the organization, they often do not come back.
The Pattern in the Exit Interviews
After Marcus left I started paying attention to exit interviews differently.
Not the formal answers, which are almost always professionally managed and rarely contain the actual reason. The moment at the end, when the interview is technically over, when the person is mentally already gone, when they say the thing they have been not-saying for however long it has been true.
That moment appears in almost every exit interview if you create the space for it. And the thing that gets said in that moment, across engineers at different levels and different companies and different tenure lengths, is almost always a variation of the same sentence.
Some version of: I did not know you saw it.
Not that they were undercompensated, though sometimes they were. Not that management was difficult, though sometimes it was. Not that the work was uninteresting, though sometimes it was.
That the work they were doing, the judgment they were exercising, the level they were operating at, was invisible to the organization in the way that mattered. Not unappreciated. Unseen. Or seen and not named. Which from the inside feels like the same thing.
The engineers who stay are not always the ones with the best compensation or the most interesting work. They are often the ones who know where they stand. Who have been told specifically what the organization sees in them. Who do not have to guess whether the investment they are making will compound or simply be consumed.
That knowledge is cheap to provide. It costs a seven-minute conversation. The absence of it costs 150,000 to 300,000 dollars in replacement, plus the institutional knowledge that leaves with the person, plus the time it takes for someone new to reach the level that the person who left had already reached.
The math is not close.
What I Changed After Marcus Left
I am not going to pretend I have this fully solved. But here is what changed in how I manage after that Thursday afternoon.
I separated visibility from communication. Seeing what an engineer is doing is not the same as telling them I see it. Both are required. I made explicit feedback about level and judgment a regular part of one-on-ones, not a special conversation reserved for promotion cycles.
I started naming specific decisions. Not “you’re doing great work” which is generic enough to mean nothing. The specific decision, the specific moment, the specific judgment call that signaled senior-level thinking. “The way you structured that database migration. The question you asked in the architecture review that nobody else thought to ask. That.” Specificity is what makes feedback land rather than wash over.
I stopped waiting for certainty before having the conversation. I had been treating promotion conversations as announcements of decisions already made. They work better as ongoing calibration. The engineer who knows their manager is building a case, even without a guaranteed timeline, has the information they need to make a real decision about whether to stay. The engineer who doesn’t know is making that decision in the dark.
I started asking the actual question in one-on-ones. Not “how are things going” which produces the professional answer. “Do you know where I think you’re operating relative to your current level?” Most of the time the answer reveals a gap between what I see and what the engineer believes I see. That gap is where the retention risk lives.
The Thing Marcus Said That I Keep Coming Back To
He said: “I just didn’t know if you noticed what you were doing, or if it was just convenient.”
That sentence describes the exact failure mode. The manager who is developing an engineer and the manager who is simply routing work
